text: The surgeon incises the posterior wall of the bladder one centimeter away from the tumor, ensuring the integrity of the tumor and avoiding injury to the bladder triangle. The muscular and mucosal layers of the bladder are sutured, followed by a running suture of the peritoneum to embed the muscular layer.

The surgical system lifts the inferior mesenteric artery, and the peritoneum is incised from the mesangial root of the right sigmoid colon to expose the space. The surgeon extends the space, exposing and protecting the hypogastric nerve. The posterior rectal space is separated, and the inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and ligated at its root. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric vein is divided and ligated. The physiological adhesion of the sigmoid colon to the lateral abdominal wall is mobilized.

The surgical system lifts the inferior mesenteric artery, and the peritoneum is incised from the mesangial root of the right sigmoid colon to expose the space. The hypogastric nerve is exposed and protected as the posterior rectal space is separated. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and ligated at the root, followed by the division and ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein. The physiological adhesion of the sigmoid colon to the lateral abdominal wall is mobilized.

The surgeon incises the mesorectum ten centimeters away from the distal end of the tumor. A stapler is inserted to excise the rectum, followed by performing an end-to-end anastomosis using a twenty-nine circular stapler.
